coredata.dc:title: High performance Pt(II) complex and its hybridized carbon quantum dots: Synthesis and the synergistic enhanced optical limiting property
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Senqiang
Zhang, Qing
Pan, Qianqian
Hu, Jinyang
Liu, Rui
Song, Guangliang
Zhu, Hongjun
coredata.dc:description: A new strategy is launched to develop efficient nonlinear optical (NLO) materials by incorporating organometallic Pt(II) complexes into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which exhibits the synergistic effect for the optical limiting performance of hybrid materials. m1-CDs were further functionalized with Pt(II) complex for the smaller sizes (2.3 nm) and more surface amino groups (54.6 %), and the Pt(II) complex also shows long triplet-excited lifetime (482 ns) and satisfactory reverse saturable absorption (RSA). The hybrid material N-CD-Pt exhibits the special π − π* transitions and broader metal-to-ligand/intraligand/ligand-to-ligand charge transfer transitions in the visible region (∼675 nm). The electron/energy transfer process occurred among the system causes a significant emission quenching for the nanohybrid, which is benefit for the occurrence of excited-state absorption process and the enhancement of the RSA. The covalent modification also endows nanohybrid N-CD-Pt with improved NLO performance, including RSA behaviors mainly from the Pt(II) complexes and nonlinear scattering responses from CQDs. The limiting threshold (F50) of N-CD-Pt reaches 0.62 J·cm−2 at 532 nm, which is better than Pt(II) complex (1.0 J·cm−2).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pt complex
Synergistic effect
Nonlinear optical
Electron/energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nonlinear properties and optical power limiting performances
Optical limiting mechanisms of the nanohybrid materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of the covalently modified nanohybrid N-CD-Pt.
Schematic diagram of distinct CQDs components on a silica-gel column.
The TEM images of m1-CDs (a, b) and m2-CDs (c, d), the insets from (b) and (d) are the size distributions of m1-CDs and m2-CDs. AFM images of m1-CDs (e) and m2-CDs (f).
FT-IR spectra (a) and XPS spectra (b) of m1-CDs and m2-CDs. The amplified C1s,N1s and O1s XPS spectra for m1-CDs (c, d, e)and m2-CDs (f, g, h).

(a) The TEM image of N-CD-Pt, the insets is the size distribution of N-CD-Pt. (b) AFM images of N-CD-Pt. (c) FTIR spectra of N-CD-Pt. (d) The XPS spectra of N-CD-Pt, (e) The amplified Pt4f XPS spectra for N-CD-Pt; (f) The amplified N1s XPS spectra for N-CD-Pt.

UV/Vis absorption spectra of (a) m1-CDs, Pt-COOH and N-CD-Pt in DMF; (b) Emission spectra of m1-CDs, Pt-COOH and N-CD-Pt in deoxygenated DMF, with the same value of the absorbance at excitation wavelength (A = 0.2).

(a) Time-resolved TA spectra of Pt-COOH in DMF, c = 1 × 10−5 mol L−1, λ ex = 355 nm. (b) Open-aperture Z-scan curves, (c) scattering response, (d) Optical limiting performance of m1-CDs, m2-CDs, Pt-COOH, N-CD-Pt and C60.
The relative contents of C, N and O atoms for m1-CDs and m2-CDs. (Determined by XPS).
The relative contents for m1-CDs and m2-CDs. (Determined by XPS).

head.ce:title: High performance Pt(II) complex and its hybridized carbon quantum dots: Synthesis and the synergistic enhanced optical limiting property

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy is launched to develop efficient nonlinear optical (NLO) materials by incorporating organometallic Pt(II) complexes into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which exhibits the synergistic effect for the optical limiting performance of hybrid materials. m1-CDs were further functionalized with Pt(II) complex for the smaller sizes (2.3 nm) and more surface amino groups (54.6 %), and the Pt(II) complex also shows long triplet-excited lifetime (482 ns) and satisfactory reverse saturable absorption (RSA). The hybrid material N-CD-Pt exhibits the special π − π* transitions and broader metal-to-ligand/intraligand/ligand-to-ligand charge transfer transitions in the visible region (∼675 nm). The electron/energy transfer process occurred among the system causes a significant emission quenching for the nanohybrid, which is benefit for the occurrence of excited-state absorption process and the enhancement of the RSA. The covalent modification also endows nanohybrid N-CD-Pt with improved NLO performance, including RSA behaviors mainly from the Pt(II) complexes and nonlinear scattering responses from CQDs. The limiting threshold (F50) of N-CD-Pt reaches 0.62 J·cm−2 at 532 nm, which is better than Pt(II) complex (1.0 J·cm−2).

ce:section.ce:para: With the wide extension of laser technology, protection from laser is becoming more and more important. Human eyes and many optical sensors are sensitive to 532 nm wavelength lasers that laser protection at this wavelength is extremely critical. Among all laser types at 532 nm wavelength, nanopulsed laser is crucial for the thermal damage due to its high energy but low cost [1]. Transition metal complexes, especially the Pt(II) complexes, are considered as the very promising optical power limiting (OPL) materials for the protection against laser radiation exposure [2–6]. Reverse saturable absorption (RSA, i.e. when the incident energy increases, the absorptivity of the nonlinear optical (NLO) material also increases due to the stronger excited-state absorption than the ground-state absorption, which is responsible for the decreased transmission) is a well-understood mechanism leading to OPL properties in Pt(II) complexes [7]. Thanking for the intense spin–orbit coupling (SOC), Pt(II) complexes could hop to a long-lived triplet state from a singlet state via an intersystem crossing (ISC) effect, which is beneficial to the RSA [8–10]. For better satisfying the increasing requirements of OPL materials, their excited state properties were tuned by structural modification of their coordinating ligands, such as the expansion of π-conjugation and the introductions of donor/acceptor groups [9,11–13]. However, the general modification usually causes a hard synthesis and poor solubility of Pt(II) complexes. So, it is a vital challenge to keep the balance between the structure and OPL performance.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) also have been concerned as the favorable OPL candidates, resulting from the presence of abundant delocalized π-electrons [14–16]. Different from the Pt complexes, CQDs exhibit the excellent OPL performance due to the nonlinear scattering (NLS) of the incident laser radiation [17,18]. This mechanism is induced by solvent nano-bubbles or micro-plasmas, which were generated from the heating of the carbon nanoparticles followed by the evaporation of the solvent [7]. Moreover, the OPL performance also can be tailored by the sizes and the functional groups covering their surface [19,20]. Although many reports have focused on the NLO characters of pristine Pt(II) complexes or CQDs [21,22], few works of the functionalized hybrid materials are reported to date.

Considering the previous reports [5,23–29], carbon-based materials can be the excellent platforms for those complexes with RSA character, which also can be used to exploit and synthesize novel OPL materials with advanced properties. For these hybrid NLO materials, their OPL properties can be ascribed to the synergistic effect among the different components, including the nonlinear absorption, nonlinear scattering and other mechanisms. Moreover, the formation of covalent bond would cause a charge transfer process in a donor (D)–acceptor (A) system, which plays a significant role in the superior OPL performance [30,31]. In our previous work [32], the graphene oxide (GO) was functionalized with Pt(II) complexes via electrostatic effect or covalent bonding. Resultant material exhibits broader transient absorption spectra and better OPL performance than the pristine ones, owing to the effective photoinduced electron transfer and energy transfer between GO and Pt(II) complex. Chen et al. also provided that during the photoinduced electron transfer process, the OPL performances could be enhanced noticeably due to the increasement of the intramolecular transition dipole moment between the excited states [33].

Based on the above reasons [34–36], in this work, we designed and synthesized a D–A system, in which the acceptor (Pt complex) was covalently attached to the surface of the donor (CQDs) via an amidation reaction, as shown in Fig. 1 . The nonlinear optical properties of N-CD-Pt have been systematically investigated via a series of spectroscopic methods, including transient absorption and Z-scan experiments. The formation of nanohybrid further prevents the intermolecular aggregation among the Pt(II) complexes, which usually adds nonradiative pathways, lengthens the excited state lifetime, and thereby increases the effective nonlinear absorption [37]. This strategy endows the inexpensive nanoparticles with outstanding properties against laser radiation in a convenient method. As we expected, the covalently modified nanohybrid considerably presents much improved NLO performance.

Pt(II) complexes with modified N^C^N ligands usually exhibit the satisfactory characters as the OPL materials, target Pt(II) complex Pt-COOH was synthesized through the general synthetic routes. [38] Synthetic procedures and details are shown in Scheme S1 and the Experimental section, respectively.
Synthesis of CQDs

The CQDs were constructed through the solvothermal treatment of m-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine in ethanol [39]. The obtained products were further separated by silica-gel column chromatography (Fig. 2 ). Two components (m1-CDs and m2-CDs) with different polarities were obtained after removing the redundant solvents.
Synthesis of Pt-COOMe

The mixture of N^C^N ligand (0.92 g, 1 mmol), Pt(DMSO)2Cl2 (0.75 g, 1 mmol), and acetic acid (20 mL) was refluxed under nitrogen for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was filtered. The precipitate was washed with water, ethanol, and ether. The crude product was further purified by recrystallization in DCM/ hexane. 0.38 g yellow solid was obtained, yield: 47%. 1H NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz): δ 9.43 (s, 2H), 8.70 (d, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 8.37 (m, 1H), 7.95–8.03 (m, 2H), 7.82 (s, 2H), 7.69–7.72 (m, 6H), 7.55–7.58 (m, 2H), 7.37 (m, 2H), 4.33(t, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 3.69 (s, 3H), 2.51(t, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 2.15–2.18 (m, 8H), 0.68–0.96 (m, 30H). ESI-HRMS: m/z calc. for C62H64N3O4Pt+ (M−Cl)+:1109.4539, found: 1109.4537. Elemental analysis calculated for C62H64N3O4PtCl: C, 65.00; H, 5.63; N, 3.67. Found: C, 65.05; H, 5.61; N, 3.61.
Synthesis of Pt-COOH

A mixture of Pt-COOMe (0.22 g, 0.19 mmol), KOH (1.08 g, 19.24 mmol) and methanol (5 mL) was heated at 40 °C for 24 h. 4 M HCl (9.62 mL, 38.48 mmol) was added slowly into the solution. The reaction mixture was filtered. The precipitate was washed with water, ethanol, and ether. 0.15 g yellow solid was obtained, yield: 69 %. 1H NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz): δ 12.04 (s, 1H), 9.18 (d, J = 2 Hz, 2H), 8.60 (d, J = 4 Hz, 2H), 8.27–8.36 (m, 5H), 8.15 (d, J = 2 Hz, 3H), 8.08 (dd, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 7.95 (t, J = 8 Hz, 1H), 7.67 (d, J = 8 Hz, 1H), 7.54–7.58 (m, 3H), 4.16 (t, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 2.27–2.37 (m, 4H), 2.11–2.15 (m, 2H), 1.95(t, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 0.58–0.96 (m, 30H). HRMS (ESI) m/z calc. for C61H62N3O4Pt+ (M−Cl)+ 1095.4382, found 1095.4389. Elemental analysis calculated for C61H62N3O4PtCl: C, 64.74; H, 5.52; N, 3.71. Found: C, 64.85; H, 5.58; N, 3.67.
Synthesis of N-CD-Pt

The CQDs covalent-functionalized with Pt(II) complex was prepared though the m1-CDs and Pt-COOH. Solution of m1-CDs (0.1 g) and Pt-COOH (0.113 g, 0.10 mmol) in 10 mL DMF was stirred under N2 atmosphere. N-hydroxy succinimide (0.013 g, 0.11 mmol) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride（0.025 g, 0.13 mmol）were added slowly, the reaction was further stirred at room temperature for 24 h. After removing the solvent under reduced pressure, the resulting dispersion was dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off 100 ∼ 500 Da) against DMF for 1 day to remove excess Pt(II) complex, and then continued to be dialyzed against deionized water for additional 1 day. Finally, the dispersions collected by dialysis were further freeze-dried to obtain final product.

The structures of intermediates and target Pt(II) complex were characterized by 1H NMR (CDCl3 or DMSO‑d 6, Bruker Avance instrument) and High resolution-mass (HR-MS) spectra (MALDI-TOF-MS). The microstructures of the CDs and nanohybrid N-CD-Pt were characterized by High-quality transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100 electron microscope) and atomic force microscopy (Veeco MultiMode3D). Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet 6700) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB250) were used to confirm the functional groups characteristics.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a, b, c and d), m1-CDs and m2-CDs exhibit the spherical shapes and monodisperse with narrow size distributions. The high-resolution TEM images and lateral size distributions indicate that the average diameters of m1-CDs and m2-CDs are 2.3 and 3.6 nm, the sizes of these particles range from 1.8 to 2.8 nm for m1-CDs and from 2.5 to 4.5 nm for m2-CDs, respectively. Moreover, the lattice fringes with spacing of 0.23 nm for both CQDs were observed, which were smaller than the theoretical interlayer spacing of graphite (002). The results of atomic force microscopy (AFM) showed that the heights of these CQDs were between 1.5 and 2.0 nm (Fig. 3 e and Fig. 3 f), which is close to 5–6 graphene layers [40,41].

As shown in Fig. 4
a, the FT-IR spectra of m1-CDs and m2-CDs described the N–H (peaks located at 3350 cm−1) and O–H (3206 cm−1) stretching vibrations and confirmed the possibility for further covalention [42]. Other peaks of C–H stretching vibrations (from 2800 cm−1 to 2920 cm−1), C-N = stretching vibrations (from 1350 cm−1 to 1380 cm−1) and C-O stretching vibrations (from 1200 cm−1 to 1250 cm−1) were also observed [43]. The elemental composition of m1-CDs and m2-CDs were further revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The full spectra depicted three typical peaks that correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s states, which provided the existence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. 4 b), the concentrations of C, N and O determined by XPS are shown in Table 1 . For m1-CDs, the C1s core-level XPS spectra could be divided into three peaks (Fig. 4 c), with bonding energies of about 284.4 (C–C), 285.0 (C–N) and 285.6 (C-N/C-O). Moreover, the N1s spectra (Fig. 4 d) reveal that the N1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks assigned to pyridinic N (398.7 eV), amino N (399.2 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.8 eV) [44]. The m2-CDs shared the similar peaks with m1-CDs (Fig. 4 d). It is noted that the nitrogen percentage of m1-CDs (13.34 %) is much higher than that of m2-CDs (8.76 %) in Table 1. According to the literatures reported [45], the CQDs diameters and nitrogen content would show great influence on the photophysical properties. The smaller diameters and high nitrogen content would cause longer excited-state lifetime and higher emission quantum yield. Moreover, the XPS results (Table 2 ) indicated that more amino groups were observed for m1-CDs (54.6 %). Compared with that of m2-CDs (38.5 %), m1-CDs with more amino groups are benefit for the covalent functionalization. On account of these reasons, m1-CDs were chosen as the platform for the further covalent functionalization.

The hybrid material containing CQDs functionalized with cyclometalated Pt(II) complexes was synthesized via an amidation reaction. Fig. 5
a reveals that the resultant material N-CD-Pt makes a different surface morphological changes from m1-CDs after the covalent functionalization with Pt-COOH. The TEM image and lateral size distribution reveal that the sizes of N-CD-Pt range from 2.2 to 4.0 nm and the average diameter is 3.1 nm, which is much larger than that of m1-CDs (2.3 nm). What’s more, from the AFM image (Fig. 5 b), it can be seen that the thickness of N-CD-Pt is about 4.0 nm, which is also thicker than that of m1-CDs (1.5 nm), attesting the combination of CQDs and Pt(II) complex.

The formation of –NH–C = O bonds between the CQDs and Pt(II) complex was a key piece of evidence that was used to confirm the successful covalent modification of the complexes onto the CQDs surface. The chemical bonding between complexes and CQDs could be confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 5 c). In comparison with the m1-CDs and Pt-COOH, N-CD-Pt exhibits the similar absorption peaks at 3317 cm−1, 2920 cm−1 and 1707 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations from the chemical bonds of N–H, C–H and C = O.

XPS spectra further confirmed the successful formation of –NH–C = O bonds, the variances between spectra before and after functionalization reveal significant changes in the proportions of functional groups. As shown in Fig. 5 d and Fig. 5 e, excepting the similar peaks with m1-CDs, two new peaks with bonding energies of about 72.5 eV and 75.9 eV were observed, which arose from the peaks of Pt4f from Pt(II) complex. The N1s core-level XPS spectrum of N-CD-Pt (Fig. 5 f) also presents a red-shifted peak at higher bonding energy (400.6 eV), suggesting that a –NH–C = O covalent bond-forming reaction has occurred between the carboxylic acid group from the Pt(II) complex and the amino group on the surface of CQDs [46]. To sum up, all the evidences strongly suggested the successful combination of Pt(II) complexes with CQDs.

UV–Vis spectra describe the ground state absorption properties of m1-CDs, Pt-COOH and N-CD-Pt. As shown in Fig. 6
a, the CQDs m1-CDs present the main absorption bands in the UV region, which attributed to the π − π* transitions of the sp2 π-conjugated domains and the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states. Pt-COOH exhibits the unique absorption peaks from Pt(II) complex. The major absorption band below 350 nm are assigned to spin-allowed 1π–π* transitions from the ligands, the broad but weaker bands at lower energy observed after 350 nm are ascribed to the metal-to-ligand charge transfer (1MLCT)/intraligand charge transfer (1ILCT)/ligand-to-ligand charge transfer (1LLCT) transitions, which is similar with the reported Pt(II) complexes [6,11,38]. For N-CD-Pt, the covalent functionalized resultant material reveals three obvious band at 218 nm, 334 nm and 384 nm. By comparison with the spectra of m1-CDs and Pt-COOH, the slight red-shifted absorption peak is due to the alteration of the ground state of the Pt(II) complex after the combination with CQDs. Besides, the N-CD-Pt also exhibits broader ground-state absorption in the visible region (∼675 nm) owing to the charge transfer between Pt(II) complex and CQDs.

The charge transfer process steeply influences the photophysical properties, especially the excited state properties [32]. For the convenience of comparison, all the experiments were run in DMF and the absorbance of the samples at the excitation wavelength was adjusted to be 0.2. As shown in Fig. 6 b. Pt-COOH exhibits the maximum emission band at 519 nm, which is ascribed to the phosphorescence due to the sensitivity to oxygen. The CQDs m1-CDs and resultant material N-CD-Pt present the obvious emission bands at 446 nm and 461 nm, respectively. By comparison with m1-CDs, N-CD-Pt shows a weaker emission peak with a hypochromatic shift of ca. 15 nm. Besides, in comparison with m1-CDs, the emission intensity of N-CD-Pt located at 461 nm was quenched significantly by 54%, claiming the electron/energy transfer process has occurred for the hybrids. Supported by the emission spectra, the CQDs act as the energy/electron donors, while the Pt(II) complex acts as acceptor. According to the reported work [8], the quenching of emission state is benefit for the occurrence of excited-state absorption process and the enhancement of the RSA.

As the potential nonlinear absorbing materials, Pt(II) complexes usually exhibit excellent RSA properties owning to their strong and long-lived triplet excited states. The nanosecond time-resolved transient absorption difference (TA) spectra (Fig. 7
a) were studied to better investigate the triplet state properties of Pt-COOH. Similar with other Pt(II) complexes [6,9,11], Pt-COOH exhibits strong and broad positive absorption from 410 nm and extends into the near-infrared region in DMF, implying that Pt-COOH shows stronger excited state absorption (ESA) than the ground state absorption (GSA) in this region. The obvious bleaching bands below 410 nm are attributed to the stronger GSA. Considering the broad TA spectra shape and triplet excited-state lifetime (482 ns for 530 nm), the transient excited states of Pt-COOH should be mainly assigned to the 3MLCT and 3ILCT states. The TA spectra results also reveal that the RSA process may occur in almost the entire visible region and part of the near-infrared region. Besides, the TA spectra of m1-CDs and N-CD-Pt were also measured, but the signals are too weak to be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonlinear properties and optical power limiting performances
ce:section.ce:para: The NLO and OPL performances of m1-CDs, Pt-COOH and N-CD-Pt were investigated through a standard open-aperture Z-scan technique, which is a conventional technique commonly used in the study of nonlinear optical materials. For the better comparison, all the samples were individually dispersed in DMF by 30 min of ultrasonic processing and the linear transmittances are adjusted to 70 % at 532 nm. C60 is used as the benchmark OPL material. As shown in Fig. 7 b, under the excitation by 4.1 ns laser pulses of 532 nm, all the samples exhibit the typical RSA when they were closed to the focus. When the Z = 0 mm, the normalized transmittances of m1-CDs, benchmark OPL material C60, Pt-COOH and N-CD-Pt at the maximal input influence dip to 85.6 %, 60.0 %, 43.2 % and 30.1 %, respectively. The achieved nonlinear absorption coefficient (β) value of the N-CD-Pt is 32 × 10-10 m/W, which is much larger than m1-CDs (1.2 × 10-10 m/W), C60 (6.5 × 10-10 m/W) and Pt-COOH (15 × 10-10 m/W). As we expected, the resultant material N-CD-Pt shows the best RSA due to the synergistic effect after the combination of CQDs and Pt(II) complex. Moreover, it is also worth noting that Pt(II) complex Pt-COOH presents better RSA than m1-CDs and C60, which is resulting from the higher-efficiency ISC yield and longer triplet excited lifetime.

Fig. 7 c illustrates the fluence-dependent scattering measurement at an angle of 40° to the propagation axis of the transmitted lasers. The NLS response is regarded as another important factor for the NLO effect of CQDs and their derivatives. For C60 and Pt-COOH, no obvious NLS responses were observed, while the nanoparticles m1-CDs and N-CD-Pt present the satisfactory scattering response.

The transmittances of these samples were plotted in DMF for better understanding their OPL performance. Pure DMF solvent displays no OPL performance at 532 nm, implying that the observed OPL response should originate from the samples. Fig. 7 d displays the transmittances with the enhancement of input energy density for m1-CDs, C60, Pt-COOH and N-CD-Pt. When under low energy laser excitation (<0.1 J cm−2), these samples keep the similar transmittances, which originates from the linear absorption. With increased incident energy, the transmission of these samples decreases drastically. When the input energy density was increased to ca. 1 J cm−2, the transmittance decreases to ca. 57.6 % for m1-CDs, 42.1 % for C60, 35.6 % for Pt-COOH, and 27.0 % for N-CD-Pt, respectively. To quantitatively describe the OPL behavior, we define the limiting threshold, F50, as the input fluence at which the transmittance falls to 50% of the linear transmittance. The strength of the OPL performance follows this trend: N-CD-Pt＞Pt-COOH＞C60＞m1-CDs. It can be clearly seen that the N-CD-Pt shows the best OPL performance, with a lowest limiting threshold (0.62 J cm−2, T = 70 %). Moreover, compared to other NLO materials at 532 nm, such as benchmark OPL material C60 (3.0 J cm−2, T = 65 %), graphene oxide nanosheets (>3.0 J cm−2, T = 70 %), carbon nanotubes (1.4 J cm−2, T = 70 %) and MoS2 nanotubules (1.1 J cm−2, T = 65 %) [47,48], N-CD-Pt still performs the remarkable achievements. Consequently, these results further demonstrate that the Pt complex functionalized CQDs endow excellent NLO and OPL performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical limiting mechanisms of the nanohybrid materials
ce:section.ce:para: The NLO performance and OPL response of the resultant material N-CD-Pt can be attributed to a synergistic effect due to the combination between Pt(II) complex and CQDs, including the RSA behaviors mainly from the Pt(II) complex and NLS responses from the CQDs. As described in Fig. 8 , when the resultant material was excited by a 532 nm laser, part of the laser is lost owning to NLS responses of CQDs. Other part could be absorbed in the ground state (S0) and excited to the first singlet state (S1), following the hop to T1 state via ISC process. When more energy was further absorbed in the T1 state and hops to the higher triplet state (Tn), the RSA would occur due to the stronger ESA than GSA. Moreover, considering the quenching of the resultant material N-CD-Pt, the charge transfer progress occurred owning to the outstanding characters of CQDs as donors and of the Pt(II) complex moieties as acceptor. The rapid transfer between CQDs and Pt(II) complex moieties also enhanced the RSA effect.

In general, nanohybrid materials containing CQDs and Pt(II) complexes were successfully synthesized and confirmed by TEM, AFM, FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy. The ground and excited state properties are studied by UV − vis absorption, photoluminescence, and TA spectra. Furthermore, the NLO properties and OPL performance were investigated using Z-scan measurements at 532 nm. The resultant material N-CD-Pt presents the synergistic enhanced OPL effect, including the RSA behaviors mainly from the Pt(II) complex and NLS responses from the CQDs. The electron/energy transfer process also improved the NLO properties. Comparing with the pristine Pt(II) complex and CQDs, N-CD-Pt exhibits better OPL performance and lower limiting threshold (F50 = 0.62 J cm−2). In summary, these nanohybrids are suitable as the potential NLO materials for the protection of human eyes and optoelectronic devices from laser radiation exposure.

Senqiang Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Qing Zhang: Methodology, Validation. Qianqian Pan: Methodology, Data curation. Jinyang Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Rui Liu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Guangliang Song: Writing – review & editing. Hongjun Zhu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20170104), Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds (2021K136B), “Six Talent Peaks Project” of Jiangsu Province (XCL-037) Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX21-1183, KYCX21-1185, KYCX21-1186) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.152567.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. J. Guo, E. X. Chen, E. Yang and Q. Lin. J Solid State Chem., 2020, 285, 121224.
Arias, I. Moggio, G. Turlakov, R. F. Ziolo, M. Biler, M. Linares and P. Norman, Adv. Funct. Mater., 2020, 30, 1910562.
W. Sun, H. Zhu and P. M. Barron, Chem. Mater., 2006, 18, 2602-2610.
G. Zhou and W.-Y. Wong, Chem. Soc. Rev., 2011, 40, 2541-2566.
